era_context: The late 14th century in Europe is marked by the Great Schism in the Catholic Church (1378–1417), the Hundred Years' War between England and France in its fourth decade, and the rise of early Renaissance culture in Italy. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty has recently consolidated power in China (1368), the Ottoman Empire is expanding in the Balkans, and the Timurid Renaissance flourishes in Central Asia. This is a period of religious, political, and cultural fragmentation and renewal across Eurasia.

events.title: Peasants' Revolt aftermath and legal reprisals in England
events.description: In the aftermath of the 1381 Peasants' Revolt (which occurred two years after this date but whose legal consequences extended backward and forward), the English crown under Richard II continued to consolidate power and suppress dissent. By 1379, the poll tax that had sparked the revolt was being reassessed, and the crown was managing the political fallout. This year marks a period of relative stability before the renewed instability of the 1380s.
events.certainty_note: The political context is well-documented, though specific events for 1379 are sparse in surviving records. The year falls within a documented period of English political consolidation.

events.title: Continuation of the Hundred Years' War: English campaigns in France
events.description: The Hundred Years' War continued with English forces under the command of John of Gaunt conducting campaigns in France. In 1379, English military operations focused on maintaining territorial holdings and conducting raids, particularly in Aquitaine and along the Channel coast. The war had entered a phase of reduced intensity compared to the mid-14th century, with neither side achieving decisive victory.
key_figures: Charles V of France (King of France)
events.certainty_note: The broader military context is well-documented; specific engagements in 1379 are attested in chronicles but lack contemporary military records.

events.title: Great Schism in the Catholic Church: Urban VI consolidates papal authority
events.description: The Great Schism, which began in 1378 with the election of Urban VI, continued to deepen in 1379. Urban VI's aggressive assertion of papal authority and his conflicts with the College of Cardinals led to the election of an antipope (Clement VII) by dissident cardinals. By 1379, the schism was becoming institutionalized, with different regions of Christendom recognizing different popes. This fragmentation would last until 1417 and profoundly destabilized the medieval Church.
key_figures: Catherine of Siena (Dominican tertiary and papal advisor)
events.certainty_note: The Great Schism is extensively documented in contemporary papal records, chronicles, and correspondence. The dating and key figures are certain.
cross_references: 1378-001 (Election of Urban VI and start of schism)

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidation: Zhu Yuanzhang strengthens centralized rule in China
events.description: The Ming Dynasty, established in 1368 by Zhu Yuanzhang (the Hongwu Emperor), continued its consolidation of power in 1379. The emperor was systematically eliminating regional warlords and centralizing administrative authority. In this year, the Hongwu Emperor was in his early 40s and had been ruling for over a decade, establishing the bureaucratic and military structures that would define Ming governance. This period saw the suppression of remaining Yuan loyalists and the reorganization of provincial administration.
key_figures: Zhu Yuanzhang / Hongwu Emperor (Emperor of China)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Ming administrative records and the official Ming Shilu provide detailed documentation of the Hongwu Emperor's reign and consolidation efforts.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans: Battle of Pločnik
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Murad I, continued its expansion into the Balkans in 1379. Ottoman forces defeated Serbian forces at the Battle of Pločnik, a significant engagement that demonstrated Ottoman military superiority and Serbian fragmentation. This victory was part of a broader pattern of Ottoman territorial gains in the Balkans during the 1370s–1380s, which would culminate in the Battle of Kosovo in 1389. The Ottoman advance was facilitated by the weakness and division of Balkan Christian states.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Pločnik is attested in Ottoman and Serbian sources, though dating to 1379 is approximate; some sources place it in 1386. The broader context of Ottoman expansion is certain.

events.title: Timurid Renaissance: Cultural and artistic flourishing in Central Asia
events.description: The Timurid Empire, established by Timur (Tamerlane) in the late 14th century, was experiencing a period of cultural and artistic development by 1379. Although Timur himself was primarily a military conqueror, his court and successors patronized scholars, poets, and artists. In 1379, the Timurid court was becoming a center of Persian literature, mathematics, astronomy, and architecture. This cultural flowering would intensify under later Timurid rulers, particularly in the 15th century.
key_figures: Court scholars and artists (names often unrecorded)
events.certainty_note: The broader context of Timurid cultural patronage is well-established, though specific events in 1379 are not well-documented. The dating is approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Pločnik is sometimes dated to 1386 rather than 1379 in Ottoman sources, reflecting uncertainty in Ottoman chronicle dating. The significance of 1379 as a specific turning point in Ottoman-Serbian relations is somewhat overstated in some Western historiography; the process was gradual. Additionally, the 'Timurid Renaissance' is sometimes presented as a unified cultural movement, but it was actually fragmented and localized, with significant variation across different Timurid courts and regions.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1379 is rich for Western Europe (England, France, Papal States) and moderately detailed for China and the Ottoman Empire, but sparse for much of Africa, the Americas, and Southeast Asia. Western sources dominate the surviving record, creating a geographic bias. Ottoman sources are often later compilations with uncertain dating. Chinese sources are detailed but focused on court and administrative matters, with less information about popular life. The year is well-documented for elite political and military history but poorly documented for economic, social, and cultural history outside elite circles.

graph_edges.note: The Great Schism that deepened in 1379 was resolved at the Council of Constance in 1417
Ottoman victories in the Balkans in 1379 contributed to the conditions leading to the Battle of Kosovo in 1389
English military campaigns in 1379 were part of the broader Hundred Years' War, which ended in 1453
